title: Limiting the Window of a Leaked Token
description:
## An Access Token Just Leaked

An **access token** for a user has been exposed. You want to **limit how long the leaked token remains useful**, while keeping the user experience reasonable. Which approach is sound?

options.text: Keep the **access-token lifespan short** so it expires quickly, and revoke the user's session / push a **not-before** to invalidate already-issued tokens immediately.
**Lengthen** all token lifespans so tokens don't need refreshing.
**Disable refresh tokens** entirely for everyone, forcing constant re-login.
Do nothing; once a JWT is signed there is no way to limit its exposure.

explanation: Two levers limit exposure. First, a **short access-token lifespan** means a leaked token self-expires quickly; clients transparently obtain new ones via the **refresh token** until the SSO session idle/max limits are hit. Second, to act *immediately*, an admin can **revoke the user's session** and/or push a **not-before (notBefore)** policy so Keycloak rejects any token issued before that timestamp — effectively invalidating outstanding tokens. Option **b** does the opposite: longer lifespans *widen* the exposure window. Option **c** harms usability with no real security gain, and refresh tokens aren't the leaked credential here. Option **d** is wrong — short lifespans, session revocation, and not-before all exist precisely to bound exposure of bearer tokens.

hints.title: Short by default, revoke when needed
hints.body: Routine safety comes from short access-token lifespans; an active incident calls for session revocation or a not-before push.
